title: Quantum corrections to the Josephson dynamics: a population-imbalance approach
authors: Oliver Hideg, Sofia Salvatore, Luca Salasnich
abstract: We investigate quantum corrections to the Josephson dynamics of two weakly coupled Bose-Einstein condensates using the population imbalance as the sole dynamical variable. Starting from the two-variable action, we derive the imbalance-only Lagrangian with a position-dependent mass and quantize it via symmetric operator ordering. The leading quantum corrections to the classical potential and mass are computed via the one-loop quantum effective action, using a covariant background-field method that fully accounts for the coordinate dependence of the mass. This yields explicit expressions for the effective potential and the effective mass, from which we derive the quantum-corrected Josephson frequency. Numerical comparison with exact diagonalization of the two-site Bose-Hubbard model shows that the imbalance-only formulation outperforms the complementary phase-only approach in the regime of strong interactions, which is the natural domain of validity of the population-imbalance description.
summary: This paper develops a theoretical method to calculate quantum corrections to the dynamics of two coupled Bose-Einstein condensates by focusing solely on population imbalance. It demonstrates that this approach is superior to the standard phase-only method when interactions are strong. The results include explicit expressions for corrected potentials and frequencies that match exact numerical results.
main_problem: Investigating quantum corrections to the Josephson dynamics of two weakly coupled Bose-Einstein condensates using a population-imbalance-only approach.
main_result: The population-imbalance approach provides a more accurate description of quantum-corrected Josephson frequency in the strong interaction regime compared to the traditional phase-only approach.
method: The authors use a one-loop quantum effective action and a covariant background-field method to derive an effective potential and mass, validated via exact diagonalization of the Bose-Hubbard model.
model_or_system: A two-site Bose-Hubbard model representing a bosonic Josephson junction consisting of two weakly coupled Bose-Einstein condensates.
key_observables: Effective potential V_eff(z), effective mass m_eff(z), quantum-corrected Josephson frequency Omega_J, fractional imbalance, and phase difference.
important_parameters: Dimensionless interaction parameter Lambda = UN/2J, total number of bosons N, tunneling energy J, and interaction strength U.
main_assumptions: Assumes weakly coupled condensates and small-amplitude oscillations around the Josephson fixed point.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares the exact, mean-field, and quantum effective mean-field evolution of the fractional imbalance and phase difference.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the derivation of a population-imbalance-only Lagrangian from a two-variable action, proceeds to compute one-loop quantum corrections using the background-field method, and concludes with numerical validation against exact diagonalization.
why_interesting: It provides a new theoretical framework for describing many-body dynamics in the strong interaction regime, offering a more robust alternative to standard phase-only approximations in bosonic Josephson junctions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The exact evolution (solid, blue) of ⟨ˆz(t)⟩and ⟨ˆϕ(t)⟩, the mean-field solution (dotted, red) and the quantum effec- tive mean-field (dashed, orange) for Λ = 1 and N = 40. Initial conditions where set to z0 = 0 and ϕ0 = 0.1. The quantum effective dynamics is more accurate than the mean-field.

FIG. 2. The exact evolution (solid, blue) of ⟨ˆz(t)⟩and ⟨ˆϕ(t)⟩ and the quantum effective mean-field (dashed, orange) for Λ = 40 and N = 40. The initial conditions where set to z0 = 0 and ϕ0 = 0. The mean-field is not shown as it almost com- pletey overlaps with the quantum effective mean-field. Instad, we show a combination: the mean-field elliptic cosine with the only-z corrected frequency (32) (dash-dot, green). The hybrid approach benefits from both the quantum correction and the elliptic correction to the time period (44). However, neither can account for the amplitude modulation caused by higher energy levels influencing the dynamics in the fully quantum case.

FIG. 3. The four plots show the predicted Josephson oscilla- tion frequency for Λ = 1, 10, 40, 80 together with the energy gap between the ground state and first excited state of the Hamiltonian obtained numerically as a function of the num- ber of bosons N. To be able to compare them, the vertical axis shows energy in units of 2J and frequency in units of 2J/ℏ. The imbalance only prediction (dashed) is closer to the numerical (dots) than the phase only prediction (dash-dot) for Λ ≲80. The mean-field (solid), that is independent of N, is accurate only as N →∞.
